title-group.article-title: Enzalutamide Combined with Docetaxel versus Docetaxel Alone in Metastatic Hormone-Sensitive Prostate Cancer: Phase III RCT

abstract.p: Background: We investigated whether combining enzalutamide with docetaxel improves outcomes compared with docetaxel alone in metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC).

Methods: In this phase III randomised trial, 1,745 patients with mHSPC were randomly assigned to receive enzalutamide 160 mg daily plus docetaxel 75 mg/m2 every three weeks or docetaxel alone. The co-primary endpoints were progression-free survival and overall survival.

Results: Enzalutamide plus docetaxel significantly improved progression-free survival compared with docetaxel alone (HR 0.39; 95% CI 0.33-0.47; P less than 0.0001). Overall survival also favoured the combination (HR 0.65; 95% CI 0.53-0.79).
Conclusions: The combination of enzalutamide and docetaxel represents an effective triplet therapy approach for newly diagnosed mHSPC.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) is a heterogeneous disease with variable outcomes. Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) remains the backbone of treatment, but intensification strategies using next-generation hormonal agents or chemotherapy have demonstrated significant survival benefits.

Enzalutamide, a potent androgen receptor signalling inhibitor, has demonstrated efficacy in the mCRPC setting. Docetaxel chemotherapy remains a standard component of therapy for high-volume mHSPC. The combination of enzalutamide with docetaxel (triplet therapy) was hypothesised to produce additive or synergistic anti-tumour effects.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Patients with histologically confirmed metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma and no prior life-prolonging systemic therapy for metastatic disease were enrolled. All patients received continuous ADT. Patients were randomised 1:1 to enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily with docetaxel (75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks for up to 6 cycles) or docetaxel alone.

Stratification factors included disease volume, prior local therapy, and planned docetaxel cycles. Co-primary endpoints were progression-free survival (PSA, radiographic, or clinical) and overall survival. Sample size was calculated to detect a hazard ratio of 0.74 for progression-free survival with 80% power.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Enzalutamide plus docetaxel significantly improved progression-free survival versus docetaxel alone (median 34.2 months vs 20.8 months; HR 0.39; 95% CI 0.33 to 0.47; P less than 0.0001). The benefit was consistent across subgroups defined by disease volume and de novo versus relapsed status.

Overall survival was significantly improved with combination therapy (HR 0.65; 95% CI 0.53 to 0.79; P equals 0.00001). Median overall survival was not reached in the combination arm versus 45.0 months with docetaxel alone at 4-year follow-up.

Grade 3 or higher adverse events were more frequent with combination therapy (69% vs 52%), including febrile neutropenia (8% vs 5%), fatigue (8% vs 4%), and peripheral neuropathy (6% vs 5%). Enzalutamide-related adverse events including hypertension and hot flushes were also observed.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Enzalutamide plus docetaxel triplet therapy significantly improves progression-free survival and overall survival compared with docetaxel alone in mHSPC, at the cost of increased toxicity. Patient selection based on fitness for chemotherapy and predicted response remains important.
